metadata.title: Build a Madison Multi-Agent Architecture Mapper with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how to build a CLI architecture mapper that classifies an AI product against the four agent meanings and five Madison roles, then outputs a failure-locatability assessment.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 3 — The AI Toolchain (Nina Harris)

beats.narration_text: Namaste — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: same frontier model, opposite products. Cursor makes senior developers faster. Devin runs tasks while you sleep. The difference is not the model — it is where the seams are. We are building the mapper that shows you where your seams are and whether failures can be found.

props.command: Classify this AI product: [PRODUCT DESCRIPTION]. Step 1: agent meaning (A/B/C/D or combination). Step 2: Madison five roles present/absent. Step 3: failure locatability (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW). Step 4: seam recommendation.
output: Agent meanings: A + B (function + ReAct loop)
Madison roles: all five absent (merged mega-agent)
Seam recommendation: separate Intelligence from Content role

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without this map. You describe your product as an agent that does everything — it researches, drafts, personalizes, distributes, and measures. Everything is inside one model call. When the output is wrong, you cannot name which part failed. Token costs are unbounded. And the brand question is unanswerable: there is no distinct thing to sell because there is no distinct thing to understand. The seam decides what customers can see, name, trust, and buy.

The theory from Chapter Three. Four agent meanings: A is a function with a prompt, B is a ReAct loop with tools, C is autonomous over a multi-step horizon, D is a specialized role in a larger pipeline. The Madison five-role model: Intelligence — the reasoning layer; Content — the drafting layer; Research — the retrieval layer; Experience — the personalization layer; Performance — the distribution and measurement layer. The three failure modes of the mega-agent: token costs scale with every role merged, failures blur because nothing is isolated, and there is no distinct thing to sell.

The centerpiece: this is the mapper's output for a single-model marketing automation system. Left column: the tool classified this product as meanings A and B — a function with a prompt that also has a ReAct loop. Neither C nor D are present. Right column: the five Madison roles — all five are marked absent. Everything is merged into one model call, none of the roles are explicitly instantiated. Bottom bar: failure locatability rated LOW — when the system produces wrong output, you cannot identify which component failed because there are no components, only one undivided call. The seam recommendation: add an explicit Intelligence/Content split as the first seam to improve locatability. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter Three.

Honesty about what this mapper does not do. The five Madison roles are a specimen, not a universal template. The goal is to teach the skill of reading any multi-agent system for its seam placement and failure locatability — not to impose a fixed architecture. A system with two explicit roles and HIGH locatability is better than five roles with LOW locatability between them.

artifactLines: The five Madison roles are a specimen, not the only valid architecture.
Two explicit roles with HIGH locatability beats five merged roles with LOW.
The mapper reads your current architecture — it does not redesign it for you.
The seam recommendation is a starting point; the human decides which seam to add first.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Multi-Agent Architecture Mapper. Four lines.

artifactLines: Architecture is a branding decision: the seam decides what customers can see and buy.
Four agent meanings and five Madison roles name the seams that could exist.
Failure locatability rates whether failures in the system can be found and fixed.
The mega-agent fails in three ways: token cost, failure blur, and nothing distinct to sell.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt into your Claude session: You are an AI system architecture analyst. I am going to describe an AI product I am building or evaluating: blank. Step one — classify which of the four agent meanings this product instantiates: A function with prompt, B LLM with tools and a ReAct loop, C autonomous over a multi-step horizon, D specialized role in a larger pipeline, or a combination. Step two — map the product against Madison's five roles: Intelligence, Content, Research, Experience, Performance — note which are present, which are absent, and whether absent roles create blind spots. Step three — rate failure locatability: HIGH means failure is localized and fixable, MEDIUM means it requires investigation across two or three components, LOW means it is in a mega-agent and the location is structurally unknowable. Step four — recommend one architectural seam that would improve failure locatability. Run this on your own AI project and look at the failure locatability rating — if it is LOW, that is your first design debt.

props.command: AI architecture analyst: classify [MY AI PRODUCT] by (1) four agent meanings A/B/C/D, (2) Madison five roles present/absent with blind spots, (3) failure locatability HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW with rationale, (4) one seam recommendation to improve locatability.

beats.narration_text: Build a Madison Multi-Agent Architecture Mapper with Claude. Liam, in for Bear.
